content:
# Genesis Chapter 20 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 11r–11v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_020_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_020_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0019

---

## A King, a Lie, and a Dream

**1** From there, Abraham traveled south toward the Negev and settled between Kadesh and Shur. He stopped for a time in Gerar. **2** There he introduced Sarah his wife as his sister. So Abimelech, king of Gerar, sent for her and had her brought to him.

**3** But that night God came to Abimelech in a dream and said, "You are a dead man because of the woman you have taken. She is someone's wife."

**4** Abimelech had not gone near her. He said, "Lord, would you really destroy an innocent nation? **5** Didn't he tell me himself, 'She is my sister'? And didn't she say the same — 'He is my brother'? I acted in complete good faith. My hands are clean."

**6** God said to him in the dream, "Yes, I know you acted in good faith — that is exactly why I stopped you. I kept you from touching her and from sinning against me. **7** Now give the man back his wife. He is a prophet, and he will pray for you, and you will live. But if you do not return her — be warned: you and everyone in your household will die."

---

**8** Abimelech got up early the next morning and called together all his officials and told them everything that had happened. The men were terrified. **9** Then Abimelech summoned Abraham and confronted him: "What have you done to us? What wrong did I do to you that you would bring such a great sin down on me and my kingdom? What you have done to me simply should not be done." **10** Abimelech continued: "What were you thinking? What made you do this?"

**11** Abraham said, "I told myself: there is no fear of God at all in this place, and they will kill me because of my wife. **12** Besides, she really is my sister — my father's daughter, though not my mother's. And she became my wife. **13** When God set me wandering far from my father's house, I said to her: 'Here is how you can show your loyalty to me — wherever we go, tell people I am your brother.'"

---

**14** So Abimelech brought sheep, cattle, and servants — both male and female — and gave them to Abraham, and he returned Sarah to him. **15** Abimelech said, "My whole land is open to you — live wherever you like." **16** And to Sarah he said, "I am giving your brother a thousand pieces of silver. Consider this a public clearing of your name — before everyone who is with you, you stand fully vindicated."

---

**17** Then Abraham prayed to God, and God healed Abimelech and his wife and his female servants, so that they could have children again. **18** For the LORD had shut the womb of every woman in Abimelech's household — all because of Sarah, Abraham's wife.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Genesis 20 — the story nobody expected to read twice

Coming immediately after the catastrophe of Sodom and Gomorrah, Genesis 20 is jarring in the best possible way. The smoke was still rising from the plain. Lot was in a cave. Sarah had just been promised a son within the year. And then Abraham — the man who stood before God and negotiated for righteous people — tells his wife to lie again.

The word *again* is important. This is the second time Abraham has introduced Sarah as his sister to protect himself. The first time was in Egypt with Pharaoh (Genesis 12:10–20). Abraham was younger then, recently called, newly set out. Here he is old, twice visited by God, richly blessed, the intercessor for cities, a man of tested faith. And he does the same thing.

The text does not explain this. It simply shows it.

---

### Verse 1 — the quiet journey south

**וַיִּסַּע מִשָּׁם אַבְרָהָם** — "Abraham traveled from there."

From where? From Mamre, from the place where he had stood and watched the smoke rise from Sodom's valley. The chapter is silent about what Abraham is thinking as he moves south. There is no stated reason for the journey. He simply leaves, traveling toward the Negev, settling in the region between Kadesh and Shur, stopping in Gerar.

Gerar was a Philistine city-state on the coastal plain, south and west of Beersheba. It is border territory — not Egypt, not Canaan's heartland. Abraham is on the periphery, between worlds. The choice of Gerar as the location for this story matters: it is not among Abraham's familiar places, and the question the chapter is asking — *does the fear of God exist here?* — is precisely the question you ask on the periphery, in foreign territory, among people whose customs you do not know.

---

### Verse 2 — the lie, again

**וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם אֶל-שָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ אֲחֹתִי הִוא** — "Abraham said of Sarah his wife, 'She is my sister.'"

The verb is bare and unhesitating. There is no narrated conversation between Abraham and Sarah before he does this, no scene of planning or decision. He says it. Abimelech's people take note, and Sarah is summoned to the palace.

The reader has been here before (Genesis 12). In Egypt, Pharaoh's officials praised Sarah's beauty to Pharaoh and he took her. Here the mechanism is identical: the king sends and takes. But now the story goes somewhere Genesis 12 did not. God intervenes before Abimelech touches Sarah. The dream comes that very night.

This is a significant structural difference between the two accounts. In Egypt, plagues afflicted Pharaoh's house before anyone seemed to understand why. Here, the intervention is immediate, direct, and articulate: God speaks personally to Abimelech in a dream, names the offense, explains the danger, and gives the king a clear path forward. The moral architecture of Genesis 20 is more deliberate than its Genesis 12 counterpart — and it is more interested in questions of guilt, innocence, and moral intention.

---

### Verses 3–7 — the dream: God speaking to a pagan king

**וַיָּבֹא אֱלֹהִים אֶל-אֲבִימֶלֶךְ בַּחֲלוֹם הַלָּיְלָה** — "God came to Abimelech in a dream of the night."

Notice: Elohim, not YHWH. Throughout this chapter — except for verse 18 — the divine name used is *Elohim*, the more universal term for God rather than the covenant name of Israel's God. This is almost certainly intentional. The conversation in this chapter is between the universal God and a pagan king. Abimelech is not a member of Abraham's covenant community. He does not know YHWH's name. What he knows — as he is about to demonstrate with surprising force — is moral obligation and a sense of justice. The God who addresses him speaks in the language of *Elohim*: the God of all peoples, the God who holds all nations to account.

Abimelech's response to the divine warning is remarkable. He does not grovel or panic. He argues. He makes a legal case: *I acted in good faith. I was deceived. No harm has been done. Would you really condemn an innocent people?* The word he uses for himself is telling — **גּוֹי צַדִּיק** — "a righteous nation." It is the same moral vocabulary that Abraham used in chapter 18 when he argued for Sodom: *Will you destroy the righteous with the wicked? Far be it from you to do such a thing!* Abimelech is making Abraham's own argument, and making it well.

God concedes the point. "Yes, I know you acted in good faith. That is exactly why I stopped you." The text here presents something striking: a pagan king being held to a moral standard, found to have met it, and explicitly exonerated. The fear of God is not, as Abraham supposed, absent from Gerar. It is present in the king Abraham thought would kill him.

The instruction that follows is clear: return the woman, have Abraham pray for you, and you will live. Abraham is here called **נָבִיא** — a *prophet* — for the first and only time in the entire Hebrew Bible. He is not called a prophet in the context of his great visions or his covenant conversations with God. He is called a prophet here, in the middle of a moral mess he created, as the reason why his prayer will have efficacy. The word is not a moral endorsement of Abraham's current behavior; it is an identification of his function and his relationship to God. His prayers work. That is what a prophet is, in one of the word's deepest meanings: someone whose standing before God gives weight to their intercession.

---

### Verses 8–10 — Abimelech's confrontation: the pagan with better ethics

**מָה-עָשִׂיתָ לָּנוּ** — "What have you done to us?"

Abimelech's confrontation of Abraham is one of the most morally bracing scenes in the Abraham narrative. The king has just been told he was a dead man. He rises before dawn, assembles his court, tells them everything. The entire royal household is terrified. Then he calls Abraham in.

His questions are precise and devastating: *What wrong did I do to you? What made you do this?* He is not merely angry; he is genuinely perplexed. He acted honorably by the customs of his people. He received a woman who was presented as unmarried. He had not touched her. And yet his household now faces divine judgment because of what Abraham did. Abimelech has more right to be aggrieved here than Abraham does, and the text is clear-eyed about this.

The phrase **דְּבָרִים אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יֵעָשׂוּ** — "things that ought not to be done" — is a weighty formulation in Hebrew. It appears elsewhere in Genesis to describe acts of serious moral violation (34:7, the violation of Dinah). Abimelech is naming what Abraham did with the vocabulary of genuine wrong.

---

### Verses 11–13 — Abraham's explanation: fear, half-truth, and prior arrangement

Abraham's answer to Abimelech has three parts, each worth sitting with.

**Part 1: Fear** (v.11): "I told myself: there is no fear of God in this place, and they will kill me because of my wife."

Abraham assumed the worst about Gerar. His self-protection was based on a projection — an assumption that pagan Philistines could not be trusted to behave morally. He was wrong. The man he thought would have him killed turns out to be a man who argues back to God about righteousness. Abraham's fear was real, but his assessment was mistaken.

**Part 2: The half-truth** (v.12): "She really is my sister — my father's daughter, though not my mother's."

This is a claim the text presents at face value: Sarah and Abraham share a father but not a mother. The lie, then, was a lie by omission — what Abraham hid was the full truth, namely that she was also his wife. The Talmud and rabbinic tradition would later debate whether Abraham and Sarah were actually half-siblings or whether this was itself a further concealment. But the text simply reports Abraham's claim without editorial challenge.

The half-truth is more troubling in some ways than an outright lie. It uses the structure of fact to deceive. Everything Abraham said was technically true; what made it deceptive was what it left out. This is a pattern the biblical narrative will return to — characters who mislead not by lying but by strategic omission (compare Jacob and Esau, Rachel and the household idols).

**Part 3: The standing arrangement** (v.13): "When God set me wandering from my father's house, I said to her: 'Wherever we go, tell people I am your brother.'"

**כַּאֲשֶׁר הִתְעוּ אֹתִי אֱלֹהִים** — "when God caused me to wander" — uses the Hiphil of the verb **תָּעָה** (*ta'ah*), to wander or go astray, with a striking grammatical feature: the verb is in the plural, taking Elohim as its subject. This is the plural-of-majesty or intensive plural construction that sometimes accompanies *Elohim*, but it is nonetheless grammatically unusual enough to have attracted ancient and modern attention. The Vaticanus LXX renders it smoothly as a singular: "when God led me out of my father's house." The oddity in the Hebrew may be pointing at the strangeness of the theological claim itself: the very God who called Abraham out also, in some sense, set in motion the conditions that led to this repeated vulnerability.

The word for "kindness" Abraham asks of Sarah is **חֶסֶד** (*hesed*) — the same word used for covenant loyalty, steadfast love, the faithfulness that holds relationships together across time. Abraham is asking his wife to exercise *hesed* toward him by lying for him. The word is not ironic here, but the application is uncomfortable. The same quality that describes God's faithfulness to covenant promises is here recruited for a deception.

---

### Verses 14–16 — the restoration: gifts and vindication

Abimelech's response to Abraham is generous to a fault. He gives sheep, cattle, servants. He gives Sarah back. He gives Abraham open access to all his land. And then — most interestingly — he speaks directly to Sarah.

**וּלְשָׂרָה אָמַר** — "And to Sarah he said."

This is notable. Abimelech addresses Sarah in her own right, as a person with her own honor at stake. He gives her brother (Abraham) a thousand pieces of silver — an enormous sum — and then says to her: **הִנֵּה הוּא-לָךְ כְּסוּת עֵינַיִם** — "behold, it is for you a covering of the eyes."

The phrase **כְּסוּת עֵינַיִם** (*kesut 'einayim*) is one of the most contested idioms in Genesis. Literally "a covering of the eyes," it appears only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. Ancient interpreters read it variously: as vindication money that "covers" or neutralizes the appearance of wrongdoing; as a public payment that clears Sarah's reputation before any who might think she had consented to enter the king's house; or as a literal veil of honor, in the sense of public status and protection.

The dynamic equivalent I have chosen — *a public clearing of your name* / *you stand fully vindicated* — aims at the likely social function: the thousand pieces of silver is Abimelech's visible, public acknowledgment that Sarah was wronged, that she is innocent of any impropriety, and that any stigma attaching to her time in the royal household is entirely dissolved. The king is paying the price of the deception that Abraham's lie forced on Sarah — the price, that is, of her reputation. It is striking that Abimelech is the one who addresses this, not Abraham.

Sarah has had the lie happen *to* her — twice now. She is the object of the deception both times, not the agent. Abimelech seems to understand this better than the narrative around Abraham ever pauses to say.

---

### Verses 17–18 — Abraham prays; the closing note

**וַיִּתְפַּלֵּל אַבְרָהָם אֶל-הָאֱלֹהִים** — "Abraham prayed to God."

And God healed Abimelech and his household. The barrenness that had descended on every woman in the palace — a judgment held in suspension while the drama played out — is lifted. Children can be born again.

Verse 18 makes explicit what v.17 implied: **כִּי-עָצֹר עָצַר יְהוָה** — "for the LORD had completely shut up every womb." This verse — alone in the chapter — uses the covenant name YHWH. The point is precise: the healing that the universal God (*Elohim*) performs is the undoing of a closure that YHWH — the God of Abraham's covenant — had imposed. The two names work together to show that the God of all nations and the covenant God of Abraham are one: the same divine presence has both closed the wombs and restored them, has both warned Abimelech and honored his good faith, has both protected Sarah and required Abraham to be the instrument of healing.

The chapter closes quietly, without verdict. Abraham prays. The household is healed. No consequences are recorded for Abraham beyond the public exposure of his actions and the generosity of a foreign king. The story moves on.

---

### Abraham as prophet: the gap between calling and character

Genesis 20 invites a question that it refuses to answer for us: how do we hold together the Abraham of Genesis 18 — who argued for the righteous before God with majestic courage — and the Abraham of Genesis 20, who lied about his wife out of fear?

The answer the text seems to give is: both are true, and both are the same man.

Abraham is called a prophet not in recognition of moral perfection but in recognition of function and relationship. God speaks through him and for him. His prayers carry weight. His covenant with God is real. None of this is revoked by what he does in Gerar. But none of what he does in Gerar is excused by it either. The text places the call and the failure in the same life without harmonizing them.

This is one of the marks of the Hebrew tradition's unsentimental honesty about its heroes. The ancestor of Israel, the friend of God, the man of faith — he is also the man who twice said "she is my sister" and let someone else carry the risk. The story keeps both truths in view.

---

### The second wife-sister episode: pattern and escalation

Genesis presents three wife-sister episodes: Genesis 12 (Abraham and Sarah in Egypt), Genesis 20 (Abraham and Sarah in Gerar), and Genesis 26 (Isaac and Rebekah, also in Gerar). Ancient readers noticed this and debated it: are these three independent historical events, or are they three versions of the same story? Modern scholarship tends toward the latter, seeing the three accounts as variants of a single traditional episode told with different emphases.

What is important for the dynamic reading is what Genesis 20 *adds* to the Genesis 12 account: the moral interiority of Abimelech. The Pharaoh of Genesis 12 is barely a character — he acts, he is plagued, he sends Abraham away. Abimelech is a speaking character with convictions and a sense of justice. He dreams. He argues with God. He confronts Abraham. He speaks to Sarah. He is, in the fullest sense of the word, a person — and a morally serious one.

The effect of this is to shift the center of gravity from Abraham's fear to the question of whether God's protective grace extends beyond the covenant community. The answer Genesis 20 gives is: yes, it does. The pagan king who acts in good faith is not destroyed. The God who appointed Abraham as prophet also holds foreign kings to moral account and honors their good intentions. The fear of God — which Abraham assumed was absent from Gerar — is present there in ways Abraham's fear had blinded him to seeing.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 11r–11v, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Genesis 20 begins in the right columns of folio 11r (page 001), continuing from the close of Genesis 19, and runs through folio 11v (page 002), where the chapter closes and Genesis 21 begins. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 15–16 (pa_0015, pa_0016); the chapter markers CAP. XXII and CAP. XXIII are visible in the left margins of page 15, and the notation CAP. XXIII reappears on page 16, corresponding to the Vaticanus enumeration system as it runs through this section of Genesis. The Vaticanus LXX text preserves the passage with no significant divergences from the Leningrad Codex tradition. No Dead Sea Scrolls fragments of Genesis 20 are extant.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
